text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic dissection of the hiatal hernia, identifying and dissecting the anterior and posterior vagus nerves. A pin rose is used to retract and apply tension to locate the posterior vagus, which is confirmed through frozen section analysis.

The surgeon identifies the posterior vagus nerve, which is located to the right of the esophagus, amidst a significant amount of inflammatory tissue. Despite aggressive PPI management, the tissue appears inflamed. The dissection is challenging, particularly on the left side of the esophagus where a mucosal tear was previously noted. The posterior dissection is less problematic, although potential adhesions are anticipated due to the presence of an aortic stent.

The surgeon navigates behind the left atrium, encountering a vessel with clear fluid, possibly a lymphatic duct. An EGD is performed, and the robotic camera is switched to near-infrared mode to assess the area. The stomach and esophagus are visible, showing significant inflammation but no evidence of leakage.

The surgeon closes the hiatus using Ethabon sutures with Pledgets, ensuring adequate intraabdominal esophageal length after anterior vagotomy. The duodenum is retracted to the patient's right, revealing the colon beneath. Co-ochorization is performed until the left IVC is visible joining the inferior vena cava. The surgeon prepares for Heineken-Michels pyloriplasty, using ICG mode to locate the common bile duct and cold scissors to separate adhered tissues.

The surgeon begins by opening the pylorus from the stomach side towards the duodenum, encountering a pinhole stricture. A mass is identified and excised from the area of the stricture, which is then sent to pathology. The mass is approximately 2 by 3 centimeters and is later identified as pancreatic tissue. The mucosa is re-approximated using vial sutures. The surgeon ensures the common bile duct is not injured and uses an EGD scope to confirm the patency of the connection between the stomach and duodenum.
